text: The surgeon encounters bowel adhesions in the right lower quadrant and begins with a limited peritoneal incision. The monopolar scissor initially fails to coagulate micro vessels. The dissection progresses towards the midline and includes limited lateral dissection. The surgeon works within the visceral plane to reduce the direct hernias, pushing away preperitoneal fat and the pseudosac, which descend without difficulty.

The surgeon identifies Hasselbach's ligament on the lateral border of the rectus muscle, highlighting the anatomy. A dissection is performed in the retropubic space, ensuring at least two centimeters between the pubis and bladder. The conar ligament is visible, along with natural fat that does not impede mesh placement. The surgeon works on parietalization of vessels and develops a pocket for mesh placement. The prominent preperitoneal fat sits over the obliterated umbilical artery, which is visible as a tubular structure.

The surgeon performs dissection on the left side, revealing the iliopsoas muscle and the iliopubic tract. The dissection continues around the obturator area to ensure proper mesh placement. The surgeon then moves to the contralateral side, confirming the absence of bowel adhesions and focusing on safe dissection in the preperitoneal plane.

The surgeon extends the peritoneal incision and performs dissection on the opposite side, carefully separating fat from the hernia space. Obturator vessels are visible during the dissection. The surgeon reorients the view to correct spatial orientation, revealing the iliopsoas muscle and achieving a thorough dissection down to the obturator region. The fat is seen overlying the obliterated umbilical artery, with careful avoidance of thermal injury due to nearby bowel.

The surgeon prepares for mesh placement by identifying the iliopubic tract and ensuring no suspicious fat is present. Bipolar coagulation is used to control microvessels near Cooper's ligament to prevent bleeding during stitching. Two extra-large meshes are placed, overlapping at the midline, resembling a Venn diagram. The surgeon uses two six-inch V-lock sutures for flap closure, sewing from lateral to medial on both sides, extending just beyond the midline.
